meta.title: Beam Deflection Formulas (Structural Mechanics Explained)
meta.description: Understand the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory and how to calculate deflection for various loading conditions. Includes UDL, point load, and cantilever examples.

hero.intro:
Structural integrity relies on controlling deformation. Deflection is the displacement of a structural element under load, and calculating it is critical for ensuring serviceability and safety in engineering design.

Strength alone does not make a good design — users feel deflection. Serviceability limits like L/360 for floors and L/250 for crane rails often govern before stress reaches yield. Euler–Bernoulli beam theory gives closed-form δ for standard supports.

The Beam Deflection Engine in AluCalc uses the same PL³/(48EI) backbone for simply supported center loads.

stepByStep: Determine the beam support conditions (e.g., simply supported).
Identify the type and location of the applied load.
Determine the material properties (E) and section geometry (I).
Calculate the maximum deflection and compare with allowable limits.
Include self-weight as equivalent distributed load when span is long.
Compare computed δ to code serviceability limit for the application.
whyThisMatters: Excessive deflection can cause structural instability or functional failure (e.g., doors not closing).
Allows engineers to optimize beam size and material to meet safety standards with minimal cost.
commonMistakes: Mixing units (e.g., using mm for I but m for L).
Using the wrong formula for the specific support or loading condition.
Neglecting the beam's own weight for very long spans.
engineeringTip: Always verify units twice; displacement units are often small and sensitive to power-of-ten errors.

calculatorCta.label: Use the Beam Deflection Calculator to model complex loading scenarios

faq.question: What is the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory?
faq.answer: It is a simplification of linear elastic theory which provides a means of calculating the load-carrying and deflection characteristics of beams.

faq.question: Why does deflection scale with L⁴?
faq.answer: From δ = PL³/(48EI) — span is the dominant sensitivity in serviceability.

faq.question: What is L/360?
faq.answer: A common floor deflection limit — total load deflection must not exceed span/360.

faq.question: When is Timoshenko beam theory needed?
faq.answer: Short, thick beams where shear deformation is significant — Euler–Bernoulli underestimates δ.

learningObjectives: Apply Euler–Bernoulli deflection formulas for common support cases
Explain why deflection scales with L⁴ for center-loaded simply supported beams
Relate serviceability limits (e.g. L/360) to user comfort and gasket integrity
keyTakeaways: Deflection often governs design before stress reaches yield
Stiffness scales with E·I — doubling I halves deflection
Self-weight and creep may matter for long-span or polymer beams
